parts.text: The sea returned a man half-dead and a chest she never asked for. Maren had spent nineteen years mending nets on that cliff, fingers scarred by salt and twine, earning just enough to keep her lamp burning and her stomach quiet. She wasn't bitter. She simply lived small. When the storm threw the stranger onto the rocks below her cottage, she dragged him inside, dried him, pressed warm broth to his cracked lips. He survived three days.

But that word — deserved — sat in her mouth like ash. I am Kunal, and I have held something that wasn't mine and watched my mind build a cathedral of reasons why keeping it was right. That private courtroom inside your skull where you play both lawyer and judge — have you sat in it? Tell me honestly in the comments what you almost kept and why you didn't.

Walked back past the crossroads until the dock appeared, old Sero still there, palms open on his knees as if he had never moved. She dropped the chest between them; it thudded like a coffin. “I almost kept it,” she said, voice cracked raw. “I still want to. Tell me why I shouldn’t. ” Sero didn’t flinch. “Because the moment you decide you deserve what isn’t yours, you stop being Maren who mends nets and start being the woman who stole from a corpse.

I am Kunal, and I once kept something that glittered too—one lie I told myself was harmless—and I still flinch when I touch that place inside my chest where it lives. Tanha dressed as fairness, upadana dressed as justice; modern minds call it cognitive rationalization, but the Buddha saw it three millennia earlier: the mind manufactures reasons the way the tongue manufactures saliva, automatically, endlessly, to swallow whatever it craves.
